authoritative_sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 is official U.S. government cybersecurity risk-management guidance intended for industry, government, academia, and other organizations, so it is authoritative for cross-industry cybersecurity outcomes and terminology.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Context framing and risk outcome alignment
used_for: business outcome and governance context
risk appetite, current profile, and target profile framing
cross-industry cybersecurity terminology and outcome mapping
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST CSF update, NIST quick-start update, or tenant source conflict

authoritative_sources.title: CISA Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA is the U.S. cyber defense agency and publishes cross-sector performance goals with NIST alignment, making this source authoritative for prioritized baseline practices and materially different critical-service failure modes.
used_for: industry-specific scenario using cross-sector critical-service language
minimum practice and measurable outcome expectations
failure modes where critical infrastructure or small-organization baseline gaps change risk treatment
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on CISA CPG revision, sector-specific CPG publication, or tenant critical-service profile change

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1 Rev. 1: Engineering Trustworthy Secure Systems
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This NIST systems security engineering publication directly addresses secure-system life-cycle activities, security architecture, requirements, design, verification, validation, and risk-informed engineering tradeoffs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Security architecture assessment and secure-system design
used_for: workflow stages for architecture evidence intake, design analysis, validation, and verification checkpoints
separation between advisory architecture guidance and engineering signoff
system life-cycle and resilience caveats
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST SP 800-160 revision or tenant engineering framework conflict

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2: Risk Management Framework for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST RMF provides the official prepare, categorize, select, implement, assess, authorize, and monitor process, which is directly relevant to control selection, evidence, assessment, authorization, and monitoring boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Control selection, evidence, assessment, and authorization-boundary review
used_for: prerequisite evidence expectations
control implementation and assessment traceability
refusal to imply authorization, certification, or ongoing monitoring ownership
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST RMF update or tenant RMF profile conflict

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-207: Zero Trust Architecture
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-207 is the official zero trust architecture reference for resource-centric trust decisions, identity, device, policy enforcement, and architecture deployment patterns.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Trust boundary, identity boundary, and segmentation design
used_for: trust-zone and identity-boundary recommendations
remote access, partner API, and cloud access assumptions
refusal to rely on generic perimeter assumptions
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST zero trust revision or tenant zero trust architecture baseline update

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-207A: A Zero Trust Architecture Model for Access Control in Cloud-Native Applications in Multi-Cloud Environments
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-207A is directly authoritative for cloud-native zero trust access control patterns such as API gateways, service mesh, sidecar proxies, application identities, and policy decision/enforcement points.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cloud-native API and microservices trust design
used_for: segmentation and service-to-service trust-boundary scenarios
API gateway, service mesh, and service identity evidence requirements
routing detailed platform implementation to adjacent specialists
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST SP 800-207A update or material platform architecture change

authoritative_sources.title: CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model Version 2.0
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA's model is official maturity guidance for zero trust transition planning across pillars and cross-cutting capabilities, directly supporting staged architecture roadmaps and evidence-gated maturity claims.
used_for: roadmap sequencing and maturity milestones
pillar evidence gap identification
confidence limits when pillar records are absent
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on CISA maturity model revision or tenant maturity model override

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 is a comprehensive official security and privacy control catalog with OSCAL representations and control mapping caveats, making it authoritative for control-to-risk rationale and evidence expectations.
used_for: control family references
evidence-quality expectations
caveat that mappings are not compliance equivalence or attestation
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST control, OSCAL, mapping, or tenant control profile update

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Controls Version 8
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Controls are recognized prioritized safeguards used by practitioners for practical, measurable cyber defense across organizations and technology environments.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Remediation prioritization and measurable implementation evidence
used_for: practical remediation sequencing
implementation group and measurable safeguard framing
small and mid-size tenant baseline evidence
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on CIS Controls revision or tenant implementation group change

authoritative_sources.title: OWASP Application Security Verification Standard 5.0.0
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OWASP ASVS is a recognized application security verification standard for deriving app and API security requirements and verification evidence while preserving separation from hands-on exploit testing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Application and API architecture evidence checks
used_for: API, authentication, authorization, session, input, data protection, and configuration requirement evidence
application architecture output fidelity
refusal to convert architecture review into penetration testing
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on OWASP ASVS release or tenant secure SDLC profile conflict

workflow_stages.name: Request intake and authority classification
workflow_stages.description: Confirm the user request, requested authority, tenant context, in-scope architecture decision, adjacent lanes, and hard refusal triggers before analysis.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Evidence and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate required diagrams, component inventories, data flows, identity trust model, control evidence, threat inputs, risk appetite, resilience constraints, tenant policy, and applicable sector caveats.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Architecture risk and boundary assessment
workflow_stages.description: Assess trust zones, identity boundaries, privileged paths, ingress and egress paths, data movement, application and API exposure, cloud or hybrid controls, monitoring dependencies, and resilience tradeoffs.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Control-to-risk rationale and option design
workflow_stages.description: Map material gaps and design options to recognized controls, architecture references, tenant evidence, assumptions, confidence, residual risk, and explicit non-equivalence caveats.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Roadmap, verification, and handoff planning
workflow_stages.description: Prioritize remediation by risk reduction, dependency, feasibility, resilience impact, tenant constraints, verification checkpoints, and named human or adjacent specialist ownership.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Escalation, refusal, and orchestrator return
workflow_stages.description: Stop or route when evidence is missing, authority sources conflict, tenant-specific obligations are unknown, the request crosses into execution or signoff, or the correct owner cannot be determined.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

artifacts: system architecture diagrams, component inventories, data-flow diagrams, and communication-path maps
trust-zone maps, network/security zone diagrams, security group and firewall rules, API gateway route maps, service mesh policy summaries, and ingress/egress inventories
identity provider configuration summaries, IAM or service identity models, federation diagrams, authorization matrix, PAM records, and privileged-path inventory
control implementation evidence, control inheritance records, exception or waiver logs, risk register entries, GRC control mapping records, and assessment history
threat model, abuse case list, vulnerability intake summaries, attack-path assumptions, criticality rating, and risk appetite statement
resilience records including RTO/RPO, backup, disaster recovery, dependency, availability, and recovery testing evidence
tenant policy, regulatory, contractual, privacy, safety, and sector obligation inventory
security architecture assessment note, control-to-risk rationale matrix, segmentation and trust boundary proposal, prioritized remediation roadmap, and escalation or refusal memo
systems_of_record: architecture repository or design review system
configuration management database or component inventory
network source of truth, firewall manager, routing inventory, security group repository, or segmentation policy store
identity provider, IAM, PAM, service identity registry, and access policy repository
cloud landing-zone, IaC, Kubernetes, API gateway, service mesh, and platform configuration repositories
GRC/control evidence repository, control catalog, exception register, and risk register
threat model repository, security review tracker, vulnerability management intake, and issue backlog
observability, SIEM/SOAR, logging, telemetry, and detection dependency records
resilience, backup, disaster recovery, business continuity, uptime, and dependency-management records
tenant policy repository and contractual, privacy, safety, regulatory, or sector obligation register

allowed: Classify security architecture review requests as in-scope, low-confidence, out-of-scope, or adjacent-lane handoff.
Produce evidence-backed security architecture assessments, control-to-risk rationale matrices, trust-boundary proposals, and remediation roadmaps.
Recommend security control placement, segmentation, identity and authorization boundaries, communication-path constraints, verification checkpoints, and residual-risk caveats.
Map recommendations to public and tenant-approved control baselines while explicitly avoiding audit, certification, or legal conclusions.
Prepare escalation memos when evidence, authority, or tenant-specific facts are missing or conflicting.
escalate: Architecture, data-flow, identity trust model, control evidence, threat model, risk appetite, resilience, or tenant policy context is missing or stale.
Tenant policy, public guidance, contractual obligations, or sector-specific rules conflict.
Regulated, financial, privacy, safety, critical-service, OT, healthcare, public-sector, payment, or contractual facts materially change the recommendation.
Risk acceptance, exception approval, production change approval, certification, audit signoff, legal interpretation, or broad architecture ownership is needed.
Adjacent specialist ownership is likely but not uniquely identifiable.
refuse: Penetration testing execution, exploit validation, scanning, red teaming, or vulnerability proof-of-concept execution.
Incident response execution, containment, eradication, recovery, forensics, malware analysis, or chain-of-custody handling.
Formal audit opinion, compliance certification, regulatory attestation, legal advice, or wording that implies third-party assurance.
Broad non-security enterprise architecture ownership, vendor selection, product roadmap ownership, cost optimization, performance tuning, or implementation approval.
Policy, governance, contractual, procurement, or risk-acceptance authorship when implementation guidance and routing is sufficient.

domain_failure_modes: The package treats cybersecurity as a generic best-practice function and omits concrete architecture records such as data-flow, identity, trust-boundary, control-evidence, and resilience artifacts.
A confident trust-boundary or segmentation recommendation is made from prose while diagrams, inventories, communication paths, and identity trust evidence are missing.
A NIST, CISA, CIS, or OWASP mapping is overstated as certification, audit attestation, regulatory compliance, or authorization to operate.
Critical-service, privacy, safety, financial, public-sector, OT, healthcare, payment, or contractual context changes the safe recommendation but is not retrieved before advice is given.
The lane accepts execution work such as scanning, exploit validation, incident containment, forensics, or production change implementation instead of routing to adjacent specialists.
Conflicting public baselines, tenant standards, and regulatory or contractual obligations are synthesized into a false confident answer instead of escalated.
A remediation roadmap ignores operational resilience, RTO/RPO, dependency sequencing, verification checkpoints, or human risk-owner signoff.
Cross-tenant SaaS adaptation leaks tenant-specific policy, diagrams, or evidence into another tenant context.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions
No unreviewed regressions and specification-based materialization readiness

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant sector, jurisdiction, contractual, privacy, financial, safety, payment, public-sector, healthcare, or critical-infrastructure obligations
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: The applicable obligation set can materially change architecture priorities, evidence thresholds, logging, retention, segmentation, encryption, access control, and approval paths.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant-specific obligation inventory and authority owner before regulated or high-risk recommendations. Escalate if missing.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific control framework mappings and crosswalk equivalence
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Public control mappings do not prove tenant control satisfaction, audit sufficiency, or regulatory equivalence.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Use mappings only as rationale. Route certification, audit, attestation, and formal compliance conclusions to control, compliance, or audit owners.

unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Cloud, identity, service mesh, API gateway, SIEM, SOAR, network, and platform implementation details can require specialized product authority.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Keep recommendations at architecture guidance level and route implementation code, production changes, or platform-specific tuning to adjacent specialists.

market_ready_research_conclusion.summary: The public source baseline is sufficient for validated spec-pack definition because unresolved jurisdiction, tenant, and vendor details are explicit runtime prerequisites and escalation triggers rather than guessed assumptions.

notes: Research constrained the pack to security architecture assessment, trust-boundary design, control-to-risk rationale, roadmap sequencing, evidence discipline, and strict refusal of execution or signoff work.
Source classes cover cross-industry operating guidance, workflow references tied to security architecture, and evidence or control standards.
